contents: GENERAL REVIEWNon-surgical treatments for hidradenitissuppurativa: A systematic reviewProce'dures non chirurgicales dans la prise en charge del'hidrosade'nite suppurative : revue syste'matique de la litte'ratureE. Robert a, F. Bodin a, C. Paul b, M.-P. Konstantinou b, Y. Gall b,J.-L. Grolleau c, J. Laloze c, B. Chaput c,*

a Department of plastic surgery, Strasbourg university hospitals, university of Strasbourg, 67091 Strasbourg,Franceb Department of dermatology, Rangueil-Larrey hospital, Paul-Sabatier university, 31059 Toulouse, Francec Department of plastic, reconstructive and aesthetic surgery, Rangueil hospital, 1, avenue Jean-Poulhe`s,31059 Toulouse, FranceReceived 11 December 2016; accepted 28 March 2017KEYWORDSVerneuil's disease;Antibiotics;Biologic therapies;Systematic reviewSummaryIntroduction. -- The management of hidradenitis suppurativa is multidisciplinary, involvinggeneral measures, medical treatment and surgery. Non-surgical treatments, often first-lineprocedures, mainly concern forms of low-to-moderate severity or, conversely, very severe formsin non-operable patients or those refusing surgery. While many treatments have beenattempted, few randomized controlled trials have been conducted, so the choice of treatmentsis most often based on the personal experience of the clinicians. The objective of this systematicreview is to propose a synthetic analysis of the currently available non-surgical procedures.Methods. -- This systematic review of the literature was conducted in accordance with thePRISMA criteria. We searched for articles in the Medline1, PubMed Central, Embase andCochrane databases published between January 2005 and September 2015.Results. -- Sixty-four articles were included. They generally had a low level of evidence;indeed, the majority of them were retrospective observational studies. They involvedbiotherapy (44%), dynamic phototherapy (16%), antibiotics (11%), Laser (8%), retinoids (6%)and immunosuppressive therapies, anti-inflammatory drugs, zinc, metformin, gammaglobulinsand fumarates.Conclusions. -- None of the non-surgical treatments can treat all stages of the disease and

Annales de chirurgie plastique esthetique (2017) 62, 274--294* Corresponding author. Department of plastic and reconstructive surgery, Rangueil university hospital, avenue du Professeur-Jean-Poulhes,31000 Toulouse, France.E-mail address: (B. Chaput).Available online atScienceDirectwww.sciencedirect.com 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.offer long-term remission. Antibiotics and biotherapy seem to have real effectiveness but theireffect remains suspensive and the disease is almost certain to reappear once they are stopped.As regards antibiotics, no association has shown their superiority in a study with a high level ofevidence. And while some biotherapies seem quite effective, due to their side effects theyshould be reserved for moderate-to-severe, resistant or inoperable forms of the disease.Randomized controlled studies are needed before valid conclusions can be drawn. In theresistant or disabling forms, it is consequently advisable to orientate to the greatest possibleextent towards radical surgery, which is the only treatment allowing hope for cure.# 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.MOTS CLESMaladie de Verneuil ;Hidrosadenite suppuree ;Antibiotique ;Biotherapie ;Revue systematique

Re'sume'Introduction. -- La prise en charge de l'hidrosadenite suppurative doit etre pluridisciplinaireassociant des mesures generales, des traitements medicaux et de la chirurgie. Les traitementsnon chirurgicaux, souvent de premiere intention, concernent surtout les formes debutantes amoderees ou a l'inverse les formes tres severes chez les patients non operables ou refusant lachirurgie. De nombreux traitements ont ete essayes mais peu d'etudes randomisees controleesont ete menees, le choix des traitements repose donc le plus souvent sur l'experiencepersonnelle du clinicien. L'objectif de cette revue systematique est de proposer une analysesynthetique des procedures non chirurgicales disponibles a l'heure actuelle.Me'thodes. -- Cette revue systematique de la litterature a ete conduite selon les criteresPRISMA. Nous avons recherche les articles publies de 2005 a septembre 2015 dans les basesde donnees Medline1, PubMed Central, Embase et Cochrane.Re'sultats. -- Soixante-quatre articles ont ete inclus. Ces articles avaient le plus souvent unfaible niveau de preuve, il s'agissait en effet majoritairement d'etudes observationnellesretrospectives. Ils concernaient les biotherapies (44 %), les phototherapies dynamiques(16 %), les antibiotiques (11 %), les traitements par Laser (8 %), les retinoides (6 %) lestraitements hormonaux (5 %), puis les immunosuppresseurs, les anti-inflammatoires, le zinc,la metformine, les gammaglobulines et les fumarates.Conclusions. -- Aucun traitement non chirurgical ne permet de traiter tous les stades de lamaladie avec une remission sur le long terme. Les antibiotiques et les biotherapies semblentavoir une reelle efficacite mais leur effet reste suspensif et la maladie reapparait de facon quasicertaine a leur arret. Concernant les antibiotiques, aucune association n'a montre sa superioriteau travers d'une etude de haut niveau de preuve. Certaines biotherapies semblent tres efficacesmais leurs effets secondaires doivent les faire reserver aux formes moderees a severes, resistantesou inoperables. Des etudes controlees randomisees sont necessaires pour pouvoir tirer des conclu-sions valides. Dans les formes resistantes ou invalidantes, il convient ainsi de s'orienter autant quepossible vers le traitement chirurgical radical qui est le seul a permettre une guerison de la maladie.# 2017 Elsevier Masson SAS. Tous droits reserves.

IntroductionAlso known as Verneuil's disease and acne inversa, hidrade-nitis suppurativa is a chronic inflammatory skin diseasecharacterized by recurrent abscesses in areas of the bodycontaining numerous apocrine sweat glands [1].Studies have reported a prevalence of approximately 1%in the global population [2], with predominant occurrence inwomen [3] (3 women for 1 man). While the pathophysiologyof this disease has yet to be clearly elucidated, severalpathogenic factors are apparently contributory: follicullarobstruction, chronic inflammation, bacteria and their biofilmand, finally, immune system abnormalities [4--7]. Thepathology is frequently associated with obesity and smokingas well as autoimmune diseases such as spondylarthropathiesor Crohn's disease [8]. The psychosocial impact of thesechronic inflammatory lesions is the main source of what canbe considered as a handicap [9].

That said, severe complications are few and far between.After several years of development, for example, chroniclesions can become cancerous in the form of epidermoid orsquamous cell carcinomas [10]. Extreme cases may bemarked by the appearance of a lymphedema, scrotal ele-phantiasis [11], urinary or digestive fistulas.Surgical treatment by wide excision of cutaneous or sub-cutaneous tissue represents the only way to eradicate thedisease [12]. As an operation applying classical techniques ofplastic surgery (controlled wound healing, grafts, flaps) [13],its main and major drawback resides in its ''heaviness'', andit is generally addressed only to the most highly developedforms.Non-surgical treatments mainly concern forms of thedisease of mild-to-moderate severity or, conversely, verysevere forms in non-operable patients or those refusingsurgery. While many treatments have been attempted,few randomized controlled trials have been conducted,and the choice of treatments is most often based on thepersonal experience of a clinician.We have carried out a systematic review of the articlespublished over a very recent 10-year period on non-surgicaltreatments of hidradenitis suppurativa, our objective beingto propose a synthetic analysis of the currently available non-surgical disease management procedures.Material and methodsThis systematic review was carried out according to therecommendations (version 5.1.0) of the Cochrane handbookfor systematic reviews of interventions and in accordancewith the preferred reporting items for systematic reviewsand meta-analysis (PRISMA) criteria [14].

Research strategyWe searched for articles published between January 2005and September 2015 in the electronic data bases Medline1,PubMed Central, Embase and Cochrane. In this research, wesued the English-language keywords ''hidradenitis suppur-ativa'', ''acne inversa'' and ''Verneuil's disease''. Thearticles were selected irrespective of language, and allresources were used in order to find a French or Englishversion of the article, or to get hold of a translation.Inclusion criteriaWe included all original articles pertaining to non-surgicaltreatment for Verneuil's disease published between1 January 2005 and 30 September 2015 and comprisingcontrolled trials and prospective or retrospective observa-tional studies involving at least four patients. Case reports,reviews of the literature, discussions, commentaries, lettersto the authors and editorials were excluded.Data collectionThe extracted data provided precise information on authors,publication date, type of study, number of cases and type oftreatment, as well as duration of administration and follow-up, modality of evaluation (scale used), results and compli-cations. Since heterogeneity and subjectivity rendered itdifficult to analyze the results, as a means of harmonizationwe devised a simple scale ranging from 0 to +++: 0: noimprovement; +: mild improvement; ++: significant improve-ment; +++: complete improvement/remission.Results

Through our research strategy, we identified 873 articles(Fig. 1). Once the titles had been reviewed, 119 referencesfor non-surgical treatments were studied and 64 articleswere finally included. More often than not, these articleshad a low level of evidence according to the criteria of theOxford centre for evidence-based medicine [15], and amajority of them were retrospective observational studies.The 64 publications represented a total number of1368 patients. An overwhelming majority of the articlesdealt with biotherapies (44%), dynamic phototherapies(16%), antibiotics (11%), Laser treatments (8%), retinoids(6%) and hormonal treatments (5%). The remaining studies(10%) dealt with immunosuppressors, anti-inflammatorydrugs, zinc, metformin, gammaglobulins and fumarates.BiotherapiesAnti-TNFa drugs are monoclonal antibodies or soluble recep-tors specifically targeting tumor necrosis factor alpha, aproinflammatory cytokine. They have received marketingauthorization according to specialty for the treatment ofchronic inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid polyar-thritis, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis and Crohn's disease;they have also been used without marketing authorizationfor a large number of other pathologies, particularly hidra-denitis suppurativa [16]. While adalimumab was awarded aEuropean recommendation in 2015 and French marketingauthorization (AMM) in 2016 for treatment of hidradenitissuppurativa, up until now it has not entitled users to anyreimbursement. And the high cost of this treatment (pre-sently 10,000 s) remains an obstacle to its generalizedprescription.Biotherapies concern the drugs occasioning the largestnumber of studies over recent years (28 out of 64) (Table 1).The most widely studied are infliximab, adalimumab andetanercept. Only four of these studies have been placebo-controlled [17--20]:

fifteen articles concern infliximab. It is administered byinfusion at a dose of 5 mg/kg at weeks 0, 2 and 6 and thenonce every 4 to 8 weeks over a lapse of time ranging from afew months to more than 7 years. Even though it yieldslittle remission, a partial response very often occurs (71%to 100%). While the one published randomized controlledstudy [20] reports only 7% remission, 88% of the patientswere responders. On the other hand, when a dose isadministered once every 8 weeks, its efficacy seems tosubside after around 8 months [21,22]. More frequentinfusions, taking place every 4 weeks, seem associatedwith better control over time [23]. That said, whiletolerance is relatively satisfactory, albeit with a fewminor infections, several severe side effects have beendeplored (one colon cancer [24], one Hodgkin's lymphoma[23], two severe neuropathies [22,24], two serious aller-gic reactions [22,24]); eleven articles discuss adalimumab. The schema appliedin France by Haute Autorite de sante (French healthauthority) consists in an initial dose of 160 mg on D1,80 mg on D15, and then 40 mg each week as of D29 withefficacy assessment at 12 weeks. The results are far morevaried than those for infliximab, with a partial responserate ranging from 10 to 100%. There exist two randomizedcontrolled studies: the study by Kimball et al. [17] is the more powerful,with 154 patients included; while the response rate wasrather low (17% moderate to significant improvement)for a single weekly dose, improvement was significantcompared to a placebo control (4%; P = 0.025), the study by Miller et al. [18] also showed significantimprovement following 6 weeks of treatments vs. pla-cebo (P = 0.024), but after 3 months, the beneficialeffects had completely disappeared. The problem ofrelapse likewise appears in the other studies, with

recurrence of the disease between 11 weeks [25] and9.5 months [26] after treatment cessation. That said,tolerance seems satisfactory, albeit with some minorinfections [17,18]; ten articles have dealt with etanercept. Usual dosage is50 mg a week during 12 weeks. Results are inconsistentand often involve small-scale series: 100% response ratesfor some patients [27--30] but 71% of poor or non-existentresponses for Lee [31] in 15 patients. The one randomizedcontrolled trial by Adams et al. showed that in comparisonwith a placebo, etanercept had no significant efficacy[19]. On the other hand, tolerance is relatively satisfac-tory, albeit with some minor infections and local reactionsat the injection site [19,28,29,31]; a 2007 study was devoted to efalizumab, a biotherapytargeting the adhesion molecule CD11a [32]. There was noresponse from any of the five patients, and the sideeffects were quite considerable (anemia, weight lossand one case of breast cancer). Having been associatedwith progressive multifocal leukoencephalpathy, in 2009efalizumab was withdrawn from the market; two other molecules (certolizumab [16], an anti-TNFalpha antibody; and ustekinumab [33], an anti-IL12/23antibody) have been tested. When consulting the afore-mentioned publications analyzing several different mole-cules, we found that only two patients had usedcertolizumab and ustekinumab, and it was therefore im-possible to draw any conclusion.

The tolerance profile of biotherapies in treatment ofhidradenitis suppurativa is generally favorable. That said,minor adverse effects frequently occur: headaches, flu-likesyndromes, tiredness, and injection site reaction. More seri-ous reactions have only occasionally been reported: bacte-rial and viral infections [26,34], neurological disorders[16,24,27], hepatitis [35], lupus [35], severe allergies[22,24], as well as a number of cancers: (breast [32], lung[34], skin [34,36], colon [24], lymphoma [23]). The degree towhich the treatment might be responsible for occurrence ofthese cancers is difficult to establish. Recent meta-analyseshave not associated biotherapies with an increased risk ofcancer [37,38]. The relevant data nonetheless confirm theimportance of a pre-treatment assessment allowing forscreening of infections or latent cancers susceptible to beingreactivated. On the other hand, no reactivation of latenttuberculosis, classically described in conjunction with anti-TNFa drugs, has been reported.From a medico-economic standpoint, it should be pointedout that in France, notwithstanding the market authorizationobtained in May 2016 for moderate-to-severe hidradenitissuppurativa in the event of insufficient response to conven-tional systemic treatment, no reimbursement is given foranti-TNFa prescription. According to the HAS, the medicalservice it renders does not suffice to justify financal coverage.Adalimumab has also received a recommendation [39]from the European Medicines Agency; dated 25 June 2015,it pertains to the extension of authorized HUMIRA1/adali-mumab use to adults suffering from moderate-to-severehidradenitis suppurativa. Last but not least, in the UnitedStates, in September 2015, the Food and Drug Administration(FDA) authorized its use in cases of hidradenitis suppurativa[40].Sbidian et al., 2015, Br JDermatol [35]467Infliximab5 mg/kg (57)Adalimumab 40 mg ev. 2w (7)Etanercept50 mg  2/w (2)

Seven articles have studied the efficacy of systemicallyadministered antibiotics (Table 2). The ineffectiveness oflocal antibiotics on deep lesions (abscesses, nodules, fistu-las) is long since established; according to Clemmensen [41],they only yield improvement for superficial pustules. Hidra-denitis suppurativa may be defined not as an infectiousdisease, but rather as a chronic inflammatory disease in whichpolymorphic bacterial flora [42] dominated by coagulase-negative staphylococci (epidermidis, haemolyticus, hominis,lugdunensis) and the anaerobic equivalent of staphylococci( propinobecterium, fusobacterium, bacteroides. . .) possessan important proinflammatory role that has yet to be clearlyelucidated.In addition to eradicating bacteria, antibiotics are highlylikely to possess a complex role as immunomodulators(mainly on T lymphocytes), which might -- to someextent -- explain their action [43,44].The diversity and particularity of these bacterial strainsimpose a broad-spectrum and combined treatment so as toavoid the development of resistances during prolonged per-iods of use.More specifically, in 2006, the association of clindamycinand rifampicin was studied for the first time by Mendoca [45]in an evaluation of this dual therapy as treatment of anotherpathology, folliculitis decalvans. Dosage was 600 mg a dayfor each of the molecules during 10 weeks. Out of 14 patients,10 (71%) benefited from complete remission with 1 to 4 yearsof follow-up, and due to diarrheas, the 4 others could notfinish treatment. In addition to these generally satisfactoryresults, other teams attested to the efficacy of this associa-tion on hidradenitis suppurativa ; out of 116 patients, Generet al. observed complete or significant improvement in 66%,with a decrease of the median Sartorius score from 29 to 14.5in 10 weeks (P < 0.001) [44]. Van der Zee et al. reportedcomparable efficacy, with a response rate of 82% and aremission rate of 47%. They also studied dual therapy dura-tion without finding any difference between a small treat-ment (fewer than 10 weeks) and treatment lasting at least10 weeks [43]. And in 2014, applying the usual schema,Bettoli reported no less than 85% of good responders [46].The main complications of this association consist in thedigestive symptoms (nauseas, abdominal pains, diarrhea)reported in 13 to 43% of patients.Another multi-drug regimen was analyzed by Join-Lam-bert et al. [47] in a retrospective study: rifampicin 10 mg/kg,moxifloxacin 400 mg and metronidazol 500 mg  3 a day,administered in tritherapy for 6 weeks and subsequently indual therapy without metronidazol as long as improvementremained palpable. While the complete remission rate wasvery good (57%), treatment duration was rather long (1 to7 months), with frequent occurrence of digestive complica-tions (64%) and candidosis.Dapsone (sulfate family) has also been studied. Its multi-ple properties are once antibiotic and anti-inflammatory.Over the last 10 years, two retrospective studies have beenpublished. In a small series (5 cases), Kaur et al. reported a100% response rate with no remissions [48]. In 2011, Yazda-niar et al. emphasized its limited efficacy, with only a 38%response rate, and no remission in 24 patients [49].
Dynamic phototherapy or photodynamic therapy(PDT)

Dynamic phototherapy consists in the association of a photo-sensitizing molecule with a light source possibly provokingcell damage. In and of itself, the photosensitizer is not toxic,but it has the capacity to absorb a photon whose length isadapted to its properties, thereby provoking a series ofphoto-oxidative reactions leading to local tissue damage inits immediate surroundings. The light source may be mono-chromatic (Laser or a less powerful light-emitting diode:LED) or polychromatic (intense pulsed light: IPL).Application of this technique in treatment of hidradenitissuppurativa has been imagined by analogy with the satisfac-tory results observed in open-label studies on PDT in thetreatment of acne, a disease with similar pathogenesis. Itsefficacy is based on the hypothesis that as with acne, folli-cular obstruction occurs in hidradenitis suppurativa. Thephotosensitizer and the light facilitate destruction of thehair follicle, thereby reducing follicular obstruction andsebum retention and possibly also having an antibacterialand immunomodulatory effect.The conventionally applied schema for acne treatmentincludes application of 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA, thephotosensitizer) as a 20% concentrate ointment acting onlesions for 45 minutes to 3 hours. It is followed by irradiationwith monochromatic red light (Laser or LED 633 nm) orpolychromatic (pulsated light) that contains in its spectruma number of photons at 633 nm that serve as activators ofALA. Sessions are renewed ever 1 to 4 weeks for 1 to2 months.The studies having applied this schema to the diseasepresently under consideration [50--53] have yielded dispa-rate results (Table 3): While Sotiriou et al. [52] et Strausset al. [53] report no efficacy, Andino-Navarrete et al. [50]found significant improvement in the five patients studied,and the positive effect persisted 8 weeks after treatmentcessation. Similarly, Calzavara-Pinton et al. [51] reported83% (5 out of 6) patients as responders, but only one of theresponses was reiterated subsequent to cessation. ALA tol-erance was relatively satisfactory, notwithstanding localreactions including pain and virtually systematic cutaneousburns.The lesser efficacy of this treatment compared to thatobserved in its use against acne can be explained by thedepth of the lesions characterizing hidradenitis suppurativa.The photosensitizer does not penetrate inside the fistulasand the red light is not embedded at more than 1 cm insidethe tissues.To avoid this handicap, Valderes-Nargenes et al. [54] usedthe classic ALA + red light combination, but treatment wasintralesional. When the photosensitizer is injected in thefistulas and light is brought into them using optical fiber, theefficacy of PDT is enhanced. Out of 27 patients, 96% wereresponders, while 78% showed significant or completeimprovement at 6 months after only one or two sessions.Other photosensitizers have been tested (Table 3):

methylene blue gel: used by ten patients, it yielded sign-ificant improvement in 80% of the cases [55]. Strauss et al., 2005, Br JDermatol [53]44Aminolaevulinic acid 20% + redlight (laser 633 nm orbroadband 570-670 nm)  1/w12Sartorius,VAS0% ++/+; 100% 0100% superficial burnPGA: physician's global assessment; PaGA: patients' global assessment; DLQI: dermatology life quality index; VAS: visual analogue scale of pain intensity; HS: Hurley stage; HS-LASI: HS lesionarea severity index. psoralen bath solution, with activation by ultraviolet A(Balneo PUVA therapy used in psoriasis treatment): Itsefficacy is low; among 13 patients, a third of the resultswere good to excellent, a third of the results were aver-age, and a third of the results were nil [56]. That muchsaid, tolerance was very good.Highton et al. [57] studied pulsed light alone in intensiveuse (2 times a week for 1 month) in 18 patients. The resultswere encouraging (complete or partial improvement: 76%),and tolerance was excellent. Lastly, Passeron et al. [58]explored the use of PDT by pulsed dye Laser (PDT) andobtained no positive result.To conclude, it is difficult to harmonize the differentresults in view of developing a relevant treatment plan. Thatmuch said, the absence of any severe adverse effect shouldallow experienced teams to apply these treatments in casesof hidradenitis suppurativa resistant to conventional treat-ments, even if the efficacy of the different PDTs has yet to beconclusively proven.Retinoids

The retinoid class of drugs comprises different moleculesderived from vitamin A (retinol) such as acitretin and iso-tretinoin. Their regulatory action on keratinocyte growthand/or sebaceous gland maturation explains their use innumerous cutaneous conditions including psoriasis and acne.The pathogenesis common to acne and hidradenitis suppur-ativa, follicular occlusion, has prompted several teams totest the efficacy of retinoids in hidradenitis suppurativapatients (Table 4).Questions regarding isotretinoin were settled for once andfor all in 2009 by Soria et al. [59]. While highly effective foracne, the molecule is almost totally ineffective for hidra-denitis suppurativa, with a mere 16% of responders among88 treated patients. The explanation of this failure is phar-macological; it is on the size and the secretion of thesebaceous glands that isotretinoin has a predominant effect;in the disease under consideration in this review, serumsecretion does not increase.As for acitretin, which has received market authorizationfor psoriasis treatment, lichen planus and severe keratiniza-tion disorders, subsequent to previous case reports over thepast 10 years it has been studied by two teams [60,61]. In aretrospective study, Boer et al. reexamined 12 patientshaving been treated for 9 to 12 months with a mean doseof 0.59 mg/kg/day. Efficacy was satisfactory: responses in100% of the cases, significant improvement in 75%, and aneffect that persisted after treatment cessation [61]. Matu-siak et al. reapplied this protocol (0.5--0.6 mg/kg/day for9 months) in a prospective study of 17 patients. Whileefficacy remained satisfactory (responses in 65% of thecases, including 53% of major or complete improvement),the effect did not prove durable [60].All of the retinoids have frequent adverse effects; whilenone of the latter are truly serious, many are quite bother-some: practically constant cutaneomucosal xerosis, loss ofhair, headaches, tinnitus, depressions and lipid disordershave all been reported, at times occasioning the suspensionof treatments. The other factor militating against the useof retinoids is their teratogenic effect, which has led to

restrictions on prescriptions for women of childbearingage. Acitretin has a rather short half-life, limited to a fewdays, but it accumulates in adipose tissue. And when alcoholis present, a metabolite with a very long half-life (175 days),etretinate, is produced [62]. During treatment and over the2 years following cessation, reliable contraceptive precau-tions are mandatory [63].That is why, in 2015, a retinoid with a shorter half-life (2--10 hours), alitretinoin, which has been given market author-ization for hand eczema, was tested by the team of Verdolini[64] in an open study involving 14 patients. While contra-ception in women remains indispensable, pregnancy can beenvisioned as soon as 1 month after the end of treatment. Itsefficacy seems comparable to that of acitretin, withimprovement in 78.5% of the patients. However, the mole-cule is pronouncedly less affordable; in France, the daily costof treatment is ten times higher than with acitretin.Anti-androgens

Hormonal influences in hidradenitis suppurativa have beensuggested due to a number of relevant factors: femalepredominance, association with the polycystic ovary syn-drome (hyperproduction of androgens) and hirsutism,diminution of symptoms after menopause and presence ofandrogen receptors in the apocrine sweat glands (Table 5).During the 1980s, several studies [65--67] highlighted thebenefits provided by cyproterone acetate and estrogens intreatment of hidradenitis suppurativa. In a study published in2007, Kraft [68] reported that 55% of women were respon-ders to cyproterone acetate.Another anti-androgen drug, finasteride (inhibitor of 5-areductase), also used in treatment of benign prostatic hyper-plasia and androgenetic alpoecia, has been studied by Josephet al. [69]. They pointed to complete remission or partialimprovement in 86% of the patients under consideration, butthere were only seven of them.Lastly, spironolactone, which is better known as a potas-sium-sparing diuretic, also possesses anti-androgenic activ-ity. Lee et al. [70] studied in 20 female patients their dailyintake of 100 mg of spironolactone, and efficacy was excel-lent: 85% of responders, including 55% of complete remissionwith satisfactory tolerance.Notwithstanding the limited populations and the diver-sity of existing molecules studied, anti-androgens seemeffective enough. However, these molecules are addressedprimarily to women; as for men, they are often subject todebilitating adverse effects (decline in libido, impotency,gynecomastia. . .).Anti-inflammatory drugs

Hidradenitis suppurativa is a chronic inflammatory diseasecharacterized by deregulation of the innate and adaptiveimmune system. That is the main reason why numerousimmunosuppressive and immunomodulatory treatmentshave been studied (Table 6).Cyclosporin A is an inhibitor of the calcineurin routinelyused as an anti-rejection treatment. It specifically targetslymphocytes, of which a large number are likely to bepresent in damaged follicles [71]. A single retrospective series[72] has explored this molecule in refractory hidradenitisFollow-up(months)ClassificationusedConclusionComplicationsVerdolini et al., 2015,G Ital DermatolVenereol [64]414Alitretinoin10 mg/d66Sartorius,DLQIResistant HS:78.5% +++/++/+; 11.5% 0NAMatusiak et al., 2014,Br J Dermatol [60]417Acitretin0.5--0.6 mg/kg/d99HSSI, HSS,PGA, DLQIResistant HS 2-3:6% +++; 47% ++; 12%+ 35% 0No long-lasting effectiveness3 severe dermatitis1 hair loss1 visual acuity decreaseBoer and Nazari, 2011,Br J Dermatol [61]412Acitretin0.59 mg/kg/d9--1248VAS, PGAResistant HS 2--3:75% ++; 25%+; 0% 075% long lasting effect(6--45 months)100%:100%Cheilitis3 hair loss1 depression and fatigue1 ear buzzing1 headache5 lipid elevationSoria et al., 2009,Dermatology [59]488Isotretinoin44 mg/kg/d8NAPaGA16% ++/+; 84% 0NAPGA: physician's global assessment; PaGA: patients' global assessment; DLQI: dermatology life quality index; VAS: visual analogue scale of pain intensity; HSSI: HS severity index; HS: Hurleystage.ReferenceAuthor, year, journalLoENumberof casesType oftreatmentDuration oftreatment(months)Follow-up(months)ClassificationusedConclusionComplicationsLee and Fischer, 2015,Australas J Dermatol [70]420Spironolactone100 mg/d33PGAFemale patient with HS 1--3:55% +++; 30% ++/+; 15% 01 dizzinessKraft and Searles, 2007,J Cutan Med Surg [68]

PGA: physician's global assessment; DLQI: dermatology life quality index; VAS: visual analogue scale of pain intensity; HS: Hurley stage; SF36: short-form (36) health survey.suppurativa in 18 patients. Dosage was between 2.0 and3.5 mg/kg by day over a period ranging from 15 days to 2 years.While response rate was 50%, only in 11% of cases did pro-nounced improvement occur, and tolerance was mediocre(digestive problems, headaches, high blood pressure, palpita-tions, fatigue. . .).Anakinra is an antagonist of interleukin 1 (IL-1) receptorsthat is used as a drug of last resort in cases of rheumatoidpolyarthritis and in a number of orphan diseases (cryopyr-inopathies). On this subject, all we have found is a series ofsix cases published in 2014 by Leslie et al. [73]. After2 months of treatment (100 mg/day), 100% of the patientswere responders, with pronounced improvement 83% of thetime. Tolerance was very good. Unfortunately, 2 monthsafter the end of treatment, all of the disease's parameters ofactivity had reverted to their initial values. Lengthier treat-ment regimens must consequently be tested.Colchicine is an anti-inflammatory drug (inhibition of thepolynuclear neutrophils by disorganization of their cytoske-leton) that is routinely used not only in treatment of gout,but also in cases of inherited diseases and Behcet's disease.Suspicion of physiopathogenesis common to these patholo-gies and hidradenitis suppurativa led Van der Zee's team totry to assess the efficacy of colchicine [74] at the dose usuallyprescribed in treatment of gout attacks (1 to 1.5 mg/day) for1 to 4 months. Most of the patients (5 out of 8) noted nochange, and the other saw only slight improvement. Inaddition, digestive tolerance was poor; in 3 out of the 8 cases(38%), discomforting symptoms appeared (nauseas, diar-rhea). Colchicine is consequently not a treatment of choicefor hidradenitis suppurativa.Brocard et al., 2007,Dermatology [75]4 22 Zincgluconate90 mg/d

were responders, with 48% of them presenting pronouncedimprovement (50% Sartorius score diminution); tolerancewas excellent (some minor digestive discomfort). Eventhough its mechanism of action on hidradenitis suppurativahas yet to be fully elucidated, its efficacy is apparently due toboth lowered insulin resistance and its anti-androgeniceffect. To summarize, metformin seems to have a positiveimpact on hidradenitis suppurativa symptoms; moreover, itssafety for use over lengthy periods of time has been clearlyand repeatedly established, a factor of major importance intreatment of a chronic disease. Last but not least, it is quiteinexpensive. However, no other study has confirmed theseencouraging results.FumaratesMonomethyl and dimethyl fumarate are fungicides used inindustry to protect manufactured products from mold ormildew. They are also used in some countries as an oraltreatment for psoriasis by means of an anti-inflammatory andimmunomodulatory mechanism. By analogy with this wide-spread form of dermatosis, Deckers et al. [77] studiedfumaric acid salts in cases of hidradenitis suppurativaaccording to the same dose-escalation protocol (from 30to 720 mg/day for 9 weeks) as in treatment of psoriasis(Table 9). The results were disappointing inasmuch as onlytwo out of seven patients experienced major improvement,while one had minor improvement and the four others eitherremained unchanged, or else suffered aggravated symptoms.Moreover, tolerance was very poor, with numerous adverseeffects (digestive, cutaneous, general. . .).Intramuscular immuno-globulins

Multivalent human immuno-globulins (antibodies) are fre-quently used in treatment to induce passive humoral immunityin immuno-depressed patients; they are also employed due totheir immunomodulatory properties in patients with autoim-mune diseases. Their impact could be of interest with regardto hidradenitis suppurativa. In his Seoul ward, Goo reviewedall patients being treated with human immuno-globulins forsuppurative cutaneous pathologies [78] (Table 10). Five hadhidradenitis suppurativa. With one intramuscular injection ofapproximately 0.15 mL/kg a month, four of them presentedpronounced improvement > 50%, while the fifth remainedstationary. Tolerance was relatively satisfactory, albeit withsome pain at the injection site. However, the high cost of thetreatment was a major obstacle. As no specific prospectivestudy on hidradenitis suppurativa has been conducted, theabove observations have yet to be confirmed.ReferenceAuthor, year, journalLoE Numberof casesType oftreatmentDuration oftreatment(months)Follow-up(months)ClassificationusedConclusion ComplicationsGoo et al., 2007,Br J Dermatol [78]4 5 IM humanimmunoglobulin1/m1--15 (3.5 m) NA PGA 80%++; 20% 0 50% injectionsite painErythemaReferenceAuthor, year, journalLoE Numberof casesType oftreatmentDuration oftreatment(months)Follow-up(months)ClassificationusedConclusion ComplicationsDeckers et al., 2015,Br J Dermatol [77]4 7 FumaratesProgressive30 to 720 mg/d7--12 7--12 PGA ResistantHS 2--3:29% ++;14%+; 57% 085% flushing57% digestivesymptoms43% fatigue+ pruritus,disagreeable skinsensation,lymphopenia,increased creatininePGA: physician's global assessment; HS: Hurley stage.ReferenceAuthor, year, journalLoENumberof casesType of treatmentDuration oftreatment(months)Follow-up(months)ClassificationusedConclusionComplicationsMikkelsen et al., 2015,Dermatol Surg [79]

removed. It also has a local hemostatic effect. All in all,Laser CO2 possesses an ablative mechanism of action iden-tical to that of surgery permitting a more targeted peripheraland in-depth approach. As with surgery, there is a singleintervention, under anesthesia that may be local, but it isgeneral when extended areas are involved. Then, lesions arefollowed in controlled healing.In three studies [79--81], its efficacy was clearly estab-lished, with 71 [79] to 99% [80] of cure. After more than2 years of follow-up in 58 patients, Mikkelsen [79] reported amean yearly 29% recurrence rate. The only side effects withLaser consisted in non-specific postoperative pain.The other type of Laser having been studied is long pulseNd:YAG (neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet). Thechromophore is melanin. It is used as depilatory Laser withthe objective of destroying the pilosebaceous follicle byphotothermal effect. Its justification resides in the follicularorigin of hidradenitis suppurativa. Mahmoud et al. [82]tested it over a month in four spaced-out sessions. Whilehe achieved no complete remission, partial improvementoccurred in 90% of the subjects, with a significantly loweredSartorius score (72% decrease) at 6 months. The only adverseeffect was moderate pain during the sessions.Association of depilatory Nd:YAG Laser with Laser CO2deroofing in two separate sessions was tested by Jain andJain [83]; while the cure rate was 100%, there were only fourpatients.These instrumental techniques seem to have some effec-tiveness, without any systemic adverse effect. Laser CO2represents an alternative to surgery; it spares undamagedtissues between the lesions and provides hemostasis forsmall-scale blood vessels. Laser Nd:YAG is relatively non-invasive and could be associated with other systemic medicaltherapies in view of avoiding recourse to radical treatment;that much said, further studies are necessary, and the mostdeep-seated lesions are more difficultly accessible to theabove-mentioned therapeutic approaches.

DiscussionHidradenitis suppurativa management is highly complex, andno single treatment successfully addresses all stages of thedisease and achieves long-term remission. Hygienic and diet-ary measures (tobacco cessation and weight loss) are of coreimportance for all patients [84].While the literature includes numerous studies and clin-ical cases, articles with a high level of evidence are few andfar between. It is nonetheless possible to draw a distinctionbetween two groups of therapeutics: well-documented treatments with several powerful stud-ies, some of which are controlled; poorly documented treatments with one or two studieshaving a low level of evidence, not allowing for a conclu-sion to be reached.Among the treatments having been studied, those mostfrequently found in our review of the literature are: anti-biotics, biotherapy, retinoids and instrumental techniquesby dynamic phototherapy or Laser.Firstly, there are antibiotics, of which the long history isclosely associated with the theory of infectious disease. A

large number of open studies have involved a sum total of244 patients [43--49]. The level of evidence for this type oftreatment is III. The most common regimen consists in anassociation of orally administered rifampicin 600 mg/d andclindamycin 600 mg/d during a 10-week cure, of which theefficacy has been demonstrated in four studies [43--46] in187 patients with a significant to complete response rateranging from 66 to 85%. However, none of these studies werecontrolled, and only the series of Bettoli et al. [46] wasprospective. Another problematic area involves drug inter-actions with the enzymatic induction effect of rifampicin,effect which may lead to clindamycin degradation [85]. Asconcerns tolerance, digestive symptoms were frequent butby no means severe. No Clostridium difficile colitis appearsto have occurred notwithstanding the frequent association ofthis ailment with clindamycin, probably due to the fact thatrifampicin generally combats this species of bacteria. Theother drug association (rifampicin + moxifloxacin + metroni-dazole) did not show any superiority, nor did dapsone. Inpractice, no high-level study as either confirmed the efficacyof antibiotics or determined an optimal association of drugs.Treatment has consequently remained largely empirical.Secondly, there are biotherapies, for which the interest-ing results are supported by four placebo-controlled studies[17--20] with 1b level of evidence and by numerous cohortstudies cumulatively involving more than 500 patients. Inflix-imab (Remicade1) and adalimumab, (Humira1) seem activewith regard to severe and resistant forms. While infliximab isgenerally more effective, adalimumab possesses a better riskprofile (no serious adverse effect), and is the only biotherapyapproved by the health authorities in European countries andthe United States. Etanercept (Enbrel1) seems less effec-tive; a controlled study did not show its superiority to aplacebo [19]. In point of fact, all published studies ofbiotherapies show a loss of efficacy after prolonged treat-ment or recurrence a longer or shorter period of time aftertreatment cessation [18--20,25].TNFa blockers may consequently be said to act suspen-sively, but the suspension can last for several months, andeven years. Their benefit is hardly negligible with regard tosevere or inoperable forms, or in the event of surgery refusal.That said, the problem with biotherapies relates to their sideeffects, and it is of primordial importance to closely assessthe benefit-risk equation and to inform the patient. Asregards long-term adverse effects, we do not yet havesufficient critical perspective. To conclude, biotherapiesshould be reserved for the most severe forms, for formshaving proven resistant to other treatments, and for situa-tions when surgery is out of the question.Thirdly, there are oral ( per os) retinoids. While isotreti-noin is lacking in efficacy for hidradenitis suppurativa, twostudies on a combined total of 29 patients [60,61] haveshown improvement due to acitretin in moderate-to-severecases. The drawbacks of retinoids comprise the usual adverseeffects (cutaneous xerosis) and teratogenicity.Fourthly and lastly, instrumental treatments have beenwidely studied over recent years. Classical dynamic photo-therapy associating a red light and topical aminolevulinicacid (ALA) seem to have been of limited efficacy in four low-power cohort studies [50--53] that included a combined totalof 20 patients. Administered in fistulas, the same associationseems to have been more effective; a single non-controlled

prospective study by Valadares-Nargenes et al. [54] involvedno less than 27 patients. As for the other combinations havingbeen studied, they are likewise lacking in conclusive proof.As reported by Mamoud et al. [82], epilatory Laser (longpulse Nd:YAG) produced rather constant improvement in22 patients, and ablative CO2 Laser possesses efficacy high-lighted in two large-scale cohorts (58 and 61 subjects) [79,80].Its mode of action seems quite close to that of surgery.The other treatments are only sparsely documented: zinc gluconate and metformin, both of which are inex-pensive and well-tolerated, could be effective with re-gard to mild-to-moderate forms, but there has been onlyone published study on the subject, involving respectively22 and 25 patients [75,76]; hormonal treatments (spironolactone, finasteride andcyproterone acetate), which are not to be used by menon account of their adverse effects on sexuality, seemrelatively effective, but there has been only one publishedstudy on each of the three molecules [68--70]; an atypical anti-inflammatory treatment, anakinra wasshown to be effective, but only on a small series(6 patients) [73]; immunoglobulins have also brought about a high responserate, but only on a small series (5 cases) [78]. Moreover,they (IM) are complicated to use and remain quite expen-sive; the other therapies (fumarates, colchicin, cyclosporin)have no demonstrated effectiveness at all. And even themost effective medical treatments seem merely suspen-sive. In order to avoid recurrence over the long haul, onlyexcision of the lesions, whether surgical or instrumental(Laser CO2) is fully conclusive.It is possible to determine a treatment plan according tothe relevant data in the literature and the existing Europeanrecommendations [86] (Fig. 2), to the severity of the diseaseand to its degree of extension:

antibiotics, retinoids and LASERs can be used duringmoderate stages of the disease, with a low-to-mediumlevel of evidence; metformin and zinc can be tested alone or in combinationwith other treatments due to their excellent toleranceand to the low degree of risk connected with their intake,but with a low level of evidence; hormonal treatments are to be reserved for women pre-senting signs of hyperandrogenism. Their level of evi-dence is low; biotherapies must be limited to forms of the disease thatare either resistant to conventional treatments or inop-erable. Adalimulab recently received French marketingauthorization in this indication; on the other hand, isolated lesions are to be treated bysurgical or instrumental exeresis; extended lesions are to be initially managed by systemictreatments.Limits of the studyThe scope of these recommendations is limited by theweakness of available proof. Existing studies are anythingbut numerous. They generally incorporate few subjects andare lacking in stratification by stage of severity; moreover,their methods of evaluation are heterogeneous and hardlycomparable. There exist few randomized controlled studies,and most published cohort studies are retrospective. Due tothe major heterogeneity of treatment protocols and meth-ods of evaluation, no meta-analysis can be carried out. In thefuture, authors should attempt to standardize their ways ofassessing response to treatment.Conclusion

Non-surgical management of hidradenitis suppurativaremains complicated in the absence of a validated effectivetreatment. While some medical therapeutics such as anti-biotics and biotherapies have shown real effectiveness, theireffects remain suspensive; upon cessation, the diseasealmost certainly reappears. As regards antibiotics, no asso-ciation has demonstrated their superiority in a study with ahigh level of evidence. While some biotherapies seem highlyeffective, the perspective of side effects is such that theymust be reserved for moderate-to-severe, resistant, or inop-erable forms of the disease. Randomized controlled studiesare needed before valid conclusions can be reasonablydrawn. As for resistant or debilitating forms of the disease,it is necessary to recommend radical surgical treatment,which offers the one and only pathway towards cure.FundingThe authors received no funding support for this article.Ethical approvalNot applicable.Disclosure of interestThe authors declare that they have no competing interest.References
